enrichment.short_description: Open-source team chat app that organizes every conversation into topic-based threads, built for focused, asynchronous communication across distributed teams.
enrichment.starting_price: Free; Zulip Cloud Standard from $6.67 per user per month billed annually
enrichment.company_size: Small team, roughly 15 people across Kandra Labs and the nonprofit Zulip Foundation
enrichment.seo_title: Zulip Review: Features, Pricing, and Alternatives (2026)
enrichment.seo_meta_description: See what Zulip offers in 2026: open-source, topic-threaded team chat with self-hosting options, Cloud pricing from free to Plus, and how it compares to Slack.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Zulip is an open-source team chat application distinguished by its topic-based threading model, which organizes every conversation into named threads within channels (called streams) rather than a single flat timeline. This structure lets distributed and asynchronous teams follow multiple parallel discussions without losing context, and it creates a searchable, organized archive of team knowledge as a byproduct of normal conversation. Zulip began in 2012 as a startup founded by Jeff Arnold, Waseem Daher, Jessica McKellar, and Tim Abbott, and was acquired by Dropbox in 2014. Dropbox open-sourced the project in 2015, after which Tim Abbott founded Kandra Labs in 2016 to continue commercial development. In 2026, Kandra Labs was donated to a newly formed independent nonprofit, the Zulip Foundation, which now stewards the project alongside Kandra Labs as its operating entity. The software remains 100 percent open source under an Apache 2.0 style license and can be self-hosted or run as a managed Zulip Cloud service. Zulip Cloud offers a Free tier, a Standard plan priced at $6.67 per user per month billed annually (or $8 billed monthly), and a Plus plan at $10 per user per month billed annually (or $12 monthly). Self-hosted deployments can run entirely free, on a Basic plan at $3.50 per user per month, on a Business plan matching Cloud Standard pricing, or on a custom-priced Enterprise plan for large-scale support needs. Pricing scales by active user count rather than feature access, and Zulip sponsors free plans for more than 1,000 qualifying organizations including open-source projects, nonprofits, researchers, and educators. Target users span software engineering teams, research groups, universities, open-source communities, and event organizers who need structured, searchable discussion rather than ephemeral chat. Zulip provides web, desktop (Windows, macOS, Linux), and mobile (iOS, Android) clients, along with a REST API, webhook integrations, and bots for connecting to other tools. Its main competitive differentiators against Slack and Microsoft Teams are first-class threading, full data portability through self-hosting, and an architecture explicitly designed for async, time-zone-distributed collaboration rather than always-on real-time chat.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Zulip
paragraphs: Zulip is an open-source team chat platform built around a topic-based threading model. Instead of one continuous stream of messages per channel, every conversation lives inside a named topic within a stream, so unrelated discussions never blend together.

The project started in 2012, was acquired and later open-sourced by Dropbox, and has since been developed by Kandra Labs and, as of 2026, the nonprofit Zulip Foundation. Because the codebase is fully open source, organizations can run Zulip on their own infrastructure or use the hosted Zulip Cloud service.

Zulip is aimed at teams that value organized, searchable, asynchronous communication over the fast, ephemeral feel of typical chat apps, making it popular with software teams, open-source projects, research groups, and educational institutions.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Zulip's core feature is topic threading: every message belongs to a topic within a stream, keeping parallel conversations legible even in high-volume channels.
It ships with desktop, web, and mobile apps, a REST API, incoming and outgoing webhooks, and a library of bots and integrations for connecting to other developer and business tools.
Administrators get self-hosting flexibility, data export, and migration tools between self-managed and cloud deployments, along with enterprise controls like SSO and audit logging on higher tiers.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Zulip Cloud has three tiers: a Free plan, a Standard plan at $6.67 per user per month billed annually, and a Plus plan at $10 per user per month billed annually, with slightly higher monthly-billed rates.

Self-hosted users can run the software entirely free, or subscribe to Basic, Business, or Enterprise self-hosted support plans that unlock features like read receipts, unlimited message history, and priority support.
Pricing is based purely on the number of active users, and the Zulip Foundation sponsors free access for open-source projects, nonprofits, researchers, and educators.

features.title: Async-first design
features.description: The interface is built to support distributed teams working across time zones rather than assuming everyone is online at once.

features.title: Enterprise administration
features.description: Higher tiers add SSO, data export, and administrative controls suited to larger organizations.

pros: Topic threading preserves context far better than flat channel-based chat once conversation volume grows
Fully open source, so teams can self-host for complete data control or move between hosting options freely
Generous free tier plus sponsored free plans for open-source, nonprofit, and education use
Designed specifically for asynchronous, distributed teams rather than always-on real-time chat
Backed by a dedicated nonprofit foundation focused on the project's long-term sustainability
cons: Topic-based threading has a learning curve for teams used to Slack's simpler channel model
Smaller third-party app and integration marketplace compared to Slack or Microsoft Teams
Mobile and desktop app polish can lag behind larger, better-funded competitors
The 2026 transition to nonprofit foundation ownership adds some uncertainty around long-term product pace

faqs.question: What is Zulip?
faqs.answer: Zulip is an open-source team chat application that organizes conversations into topic-based threads, designed to keep distributed and asynchronous teams focused and organized.

faqs.question: Is Zulip free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes. Zulip offers a free Zulip Cloud tier and a completely free self-hosted option, alongside paid Cloud and self-hosted plans with additional features and support.

faqs.question: Can I self-host Zulip?
faqs.answer: Yes. Zulip's full codebase is open source, so organizations can deploy and manage their own Zulip server instead of using the hosted Cloud service.

faqs.question: How is Zulip different from Slack?
faqs.answer: Zulip's defining difference is topic-based threading, where every message belongs to a named topic within a channel, which keeps parallel conversations organized in a way flat channel chat does not.

faqs.question: Who owns Zulip now?
faqs.answer: Zulip is stewarded by the nonprofit Zulip Foundation, formed in 2026, with Kandra Labs continuing as the primary development team.

faqs.question: Does Zulip offer free plans for nonprofits or open source projects?
faqs.answer: Yes. Zulip sponsors free Cloud plans for more than 1,000 qualifying organizations, including open-source projects, educators, researchers, and nonprofits.

faqs.question: What platforms does Zulip support?
faqs.answer: Zulip provides apps for web browsers, Windows, macOS, Linux, iOS, and Android.

faqs.question: Does Zulip integrate with other tools?
faqs.answer: Yes. Zulip offers a REST API, incoming and outgoing webhooks, and a library of bots for integrating with other developer and business tools.

enrichment.short_description: Enterprise subscription management and monetization platform covering billing, revenue recognition, payments, and CPQ for recurring-revenue businesses.
enrichment.seo_title: Zuora Review: Subscription Billing and Revenue Platform (2026)
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Explore Zuora's subscription billing, revenue recognition, payments, and CPQ platform for enterprises, including features, pricing tiers, and use cases.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Zuora is an enterprise subscription management and monetization platform founded in 2007 by Tien Tzuo along with former WebEx engineers K.V. Rao and Cheng Zou. The company is headquartered in Redwood City, California, and employs roughly 1,600 people across 29 global locations, with the largest concentrations of staff in Redwood City, San Francisco, and Atlanta. Zuora is publicly traded and serves more than 1,000 companies worldwide, including well-known names such as Zoom, Asana, Ford, Zendesk, Okta, and Salesforce. The platform covers the full quote-to-cash lifecycle for subscription and usage-based businesses through several integrated products. Zuora Billing handles flexible recurring, usage-based, and hybrid pricing models. Zuora Revenue automates revenue recognition to help finance teams stay compliant with standards like ASC 606 and IFRS 15. Zuora Payments provides global payment processing with built-in fraud protection, while Zuora Collections uses AI to improve dunning and cash recovery. Zuora CPQ, which integrates with Salesforce, streamlines configure-price-quote workflows for sales teams selling recurring contracts, and Zuora AI Paywalls lets teams personalize subscriber experiences without custom code. The company has increasingly emphasized AI features across its suite to reduce manual billing operations work, speed up implementation, and support complex, granular pricing structures. Zuora does not publish self-serve pricing; instead, it sells through a sales-led, quote-based model with contracts customized around a customer's projected billing volume, subscriber count, contract length, and which modules are bundled in. Third-party pricing trackers describe roughly three tiers in Zuora's go-to-market motion: an entry tier aimed at startups and MVPs launching a subscription product, a mid-tier aimed at scale-ups needing usage-based pricing, Salesforce CPQ integration, and higher billing capacity, and an enterprise tier for global, high-volume, or multi-brand operations with unlimited usage-based pricing support. Multi-year contract discounts are common. Zuora is best suited to mid-market and enterprise SaaS and subscription businesses with complex monetization needs, and it is regularly recognized as a leader in analyst reports such as the Gartner Magic Quadrant and Forrester Wave for subscription management and recurring billing platforms. Because of its quote-based pricing and implementation complexity, it tends to be a poor fit for very small businesses or teams needing a lightweight, self-serve billing tool.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Zuora
paragraphs: Zuora is an enterprise software platform built to help subscription and recurring-revenue businesses manage the entire quote-to-cash process, from pricing and quoting through billing, payments, collections, and revenue recognition.

Founded in 2007 and headquartered in Redwood City, California, Zuora has grown into a publicly traded company serving over 1,000 customers worldwide, including major names like Zoom, Ford, Okta, and Salesforce.

The platform is aimed primarily at finance and revenue operations leaders in SaaS and subscription businesses who need to support complex, evolving pricing models without stitching together multiple point solutions.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Zuora Billing supports subscription, usage-based, and hybrid pricing models with the flexibility to change pricing structures as a business evolves.
Zuora Revenue automates revenue recognition for GAAP and IFRS compliance, while Zuora Payments and Zuora Collections handle global payment processing and AI-assisted cash recovery.
Zuora CPQ integrates with Salesforce to connect sales quoting directly to billing, and newer AI Paywalls tools let teams personalize subscriber offers without engineering support.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Zuora does not publish list pricing. Costs are determined through a sales quote based on factors like billing volume, subscriber count, contract length, and which product modules are included.

Third-party analyses describe an entry-level tier for startups and MVPs, a mid tier adding usage-based pricing and Salesforce CPQ integration for scale-ups, and an enterprise tier supporting unlimited usage-based billing for global operations.
Multi-year commitments typically come with discounted pricing, and total cost of ownership can be substantial for large enterprise deployments given implementation and support requirements.

Strong support for complex, usage-based, and hybrid pricing models
Recognized as a leader by major analyst firms for subscription management

faqs.question: What is Zuora used for?
faqs.answer: Zuora is used by subscription and recurring-revenue businesses to manage billing, payments, revenue recognition, and quoting across the customer lifecycle.

faqs.question: How much does Zuora cost?
faqs.answer: Zuora does not publish list pricing; costs are set through a custom sales quote based on billing volume, subscriber count, and which modules a company needs.

faqs.question: What are Zuora's main products?
faqs.answer: Zuora's core products are Zuora Billing, Zuora Revenue, Zuora Payments, Zuora Collections, Zuora CPQ, and Zuora AI Paywalls.

faqs.question: Who uses Zuora?
faqs.answer: Zuora is used by mid-market and enterprise subscription businesses, including well-known companies like Zoom, Ford, Okta, and Salesforce.

faqs.answer: Yes. Zuora natively supports usage-based and hybrid pricing models alongside traditional flat subscriptions.

faqs.answer: Zuora is generally built for mid-market and enterprise companies with complex billing needs; smaller businesses may find lighter, self-serve billing tools a better fit.

faqs.answer: Yes. Zuora CPQ integrates directly with Salesforce to connect sales quoting with billing and revenue processes.

faqs.answer: Zuora was founded in 2007 by Tien Tzuo along with K.V. Rao and Cheng Zou.

enrichment.short_description: Cloud-based drag-and-drop form builder for creating surveys, registrations, and payment forms, with HIPAA and GDPR compliant data handling for regulated industries.
enrichment.starting_price: Free plan available; paid plans start at $37 per month (Gold)
enrichment.seo_meta_description: See what 123FormBuilder offers in 2026: drag-and-drop online forms, HIPAA and GDPR compliance, payment integrations, and pricing plans from free to enterprise.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: 123FormBuilder is a cloud-based online form builder that lets businesses create surveys, registration forms, order forms, and payment forms without coding. It was founded in 2008 in Timișoara, Romania by Florin Cornianu and Tudor Bastea, originally under the name 123ContactForm, and rebranded to 123FormBuilder in late 2017. The company grew steadily from its early years, reporting 100 percent year-on-year growth in its first years and reaching hundreds of thousands of users by the mid-2010s. It has since been acquired by Kiteworks, a content governance and secure file-sharing company, and now operates as part of Kiteworks' broader compliance-focused product portfolio while retaining its own branding and platform. 123FormBuilder is used by more than 35,000 organizations, including large enterprises like Airbus, LG, and the University of Oxford. The platform's core strengths are its drag-and-drop form editor, a library of more than 3,000 prebuilt templates spanning industries and use cases, and support for conditional logic that lets forms branch dynamically based on user responses. It supports payment collection through processors including PayPal, Stripe, and Square, and connects to more than 4,000 third-party tools through native integrations and Zapier. Because of its Kiteworks ownership, 123FormBuilder places heavy emphasis on security and regulatory compliance, offering HIPAA and GDPR certified data handling, 256-bit SSL encryption, single sign-on, custom roles, audit trails, and WCAG 2.1 accessibility support, making it a common choice for healthcare, education, and government organizations that need to collect sensitive data safely. Pricing follows a freemium model: a free Basic plan covers basic form creation with submission limits, while paid tiers named Gold, Platinum, and Diamond add features like more submissions, advanced integrations, and expanded storage, with an Enterprise tier available on a custom annual quote for large organizations needing dedicated compliance and support features. 123FormBuilder competes with form builders like Typeform, JotForm, and Google Forms, but differentiates itself primarily through its compliance certifications and large integration and template ecosystem, making it particularly well suited for regulated industries that need auditable, secure data collection rather than the fastest or most design-forward form-building experience.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is 123FormBuilder
paragraphs: 123FormBuilder is a cloud-based platform for building online forms, surveys, and payment collection pages without writing code. It was founded in 2008 in Timișoara, Romania, originally under the name 123ContactForm.

The company rebranded to 123FormBuilder in 2017 and has since been acquired by Kiteworks, a secure content and file-sharing company, giving the platform a strong emphasis on compliance and data security alongside its form-building tools.
It is used by more than 35,000 organizations worldwide, including enterprises like Airbus and the University of Oxford, spanning use cases from marketing lead capture to healthcare intake forms.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The platform offers a drag-and-drop form editor with more than 3,000 templates, conditional logic for multi-step and branching forms, and mobile-responsive form output.
Payment collection is built in through PayPal, Stripe, Square, and other processors, and forms can connect to more than 4,000 external tools through native integrations or Zapier.
Security and compliance are a major focus, with HIPAA and GDPR certified data handling, SSL encryption, single sign-on, audit trails, and WCAG 2.1 accessibility support available on the platform.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: 123FormBuilder offers a free Basic plan for simple forms with limited submissions, alongside paid tiers named Gold, Platinum, and Diamond that unlock higher submission limits, more integrations, and expanded storage.
The Gold plan starts at $37 per month, with Platinum priced around $49 per month and Diamond around $99 per month, billed monthly.
An Enterprise tier is available for large organizations that need dedicated compliance features and support, priced through a custom annual quote.

Strong compliance credentials, including HIPAA and GDPR, for regulated industries

Handles complex, multi-step forms with conditional logic well
Free plan available for basic use before committing to a paid tier
cons: Paid plans get relatively expensive quickly once advanced features are needed
Interface can feel less modern than some newer, design-forward form builder competitors
Enterprise pricing requires a custom quote, reducing upfront pricing transparency

enrichment.short_description: Former social-interest mobile advertising and audience-targeting platform, acquired by AcuityAds in 2016 and now folded into the illumin advertising platform rather than operating as an independent product.
enrichment.headquarters: San Francisco, California, United States (historical, prior to acquisition)
enrichment.seo_title: 140 Proof: What Happened to This Ad-Tech Platform (2026 Update)
enrichment.seo_meta_description: 140 Proof was a social-interest mobile ad targeting company acquired by AcuityAds in 2016. See its history, what it did, and its status today under illumin.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: 140 Proof was an advertising technology company launched in 2009 by Jon Elvekrog and John Manoogian III, headquartered in San Francisco, California. The company built patented content-matching technology that aggregated social data from platforms like Twitter and Foursquare into what it called a Blended Interest Graph, using that data to power interest-based mobile and social advertising targeting and recommendations for brands. It raised venture funding, including backing from BlueRun Ventures, and operated as a roughly 20-person team at the time of its acquisition. In August 2016, 140 Proof was acquired by AcuityAds, a Canadian advertising technology company, in an all-cash deal worth up to $20 million, structured with an initial payment plus earn-outs tied to performance. Following the acquisition, 140 Proof's leadership and team joined AcuityAds, and its targeting technology was absorbed into AcuityAds' broader advertising platform rather than continuing to operate as a standalone, separately branded product. In 2023, AcuityAds itself fully rebranded to illumin, retaining AcuityAds Holdings Inc. as its legal corporate name while doing business publicly as illumin, a self-serve, journey-based demand-side advertising platform. As of the current research, the 140proof.com domain no longer hosts an independent product; it redirects through illumin's parent corporate site, and there is no evidence that the 140 Proof brand is still marketed or sold as a distinct product. This tool listing is therefore historical in nature: 140 Proof represents a notable piece of 2010s-era social and mobile ad-tech history, illustrating the interest-graph approach to audience targeting that was common before increased platform data restrictions and privacy regulation reshaped the ad-tech industry, but it is not something a business could sign up for or purchase today as a standalone product. Anyone looking for its modern equivalent functionality, such as interest and behavioral targeting for social and mobile advertising, would need to evaluate illumin directly or other current demand-side platforms and social ad-targeting tools, since 140 Proof's original technology and team now operate under that later corporate identity rather than under the 140 Proof name.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Was 140 Proof
paragraphs: 140 Proof was an advertising technology company founded in 2009 in San Francisco, California, that specialized in social and mobile ad targeting based on users' interests inferred from social media activity.

Its core technology, sometimes referred to as a Blended Interest Graph, aggregated social signals from platforms like Twitter and Foursquare to help brands target ads and recommendations based on inferred consumer interests.

The company was acquired by AcuityAds in 2016 for up to $20 million, and its team and technology were integrated into AcuityAds' broader advertising platform rather than continuing as an independently branded product.

long_form_blocks.heading: What It Offered Historically
paragraphs: 140 Proof's core offering was interest-based ad targeting, using patented content-matching technology to connect brands with relevant audiences based on social data rather than traditional demographic targeting alone.
It served advertisers and publishers looking to reach mobile and social media users with more relevant ad creative and recommendations.
Following its acquisition, these targeting capabilities were absorbed into AcuityAds' platform, which itself later rebranded fully to illumin in 2023.

long_form_blocks.heading: Current Status and Pricing
paragraphs: 140 Proof does not operate as an independent, purchasable product today, so there is no current public pricing to report.
The 140proof.com domain redirects through pages associated with its acquirer's corporate site rather than hosting a standalone product or signup flow.
Businesses seeking similar interest-based social and mobile ad targeting capabilities today would need to evaluate illumin or other current demand-side advertising platforms directly.

features.title: Acquired technology base
features.description: Core targeting technology was absorbed into AcuityAds' (now illumin's) advertising platform after acquisition.

pros: Historically notable for pioneering interest-graph based social ad targeting in the early 2010s
Backed by venture investors and acquired at a meaningful valuation, indicating real market traction at the time
Its technology and team continued to be used within its acquirer's platform rather than being shut down outright
cons: No longer operates or is sold as an independent, standalone product
Its official domain no longer hosts current company information or a live signup flow
Modern buyers cannot evaluate or purchase 140 Proof directly, only its successor platform, illumin
Public information about the brand is largely historical, with limited current documentation

faqs.question: What was 140 Proof?
faqs.answer: 140 Proof was a social and mobile advertising technology company founded in 2009 that used an interest graph built from social data to power ad targeting and brand recommendations.

faqs.question: Is 140 Proof still an active company?
faqs.answer: No. 140 Proof was acquired by AcuityAds in 2016, and its technology and team were absorbed into AcuityAds, which itself rebranded to illumin in 2023. It does not operate as a standalone product today.

faqs.question: Who acquired 140 Proof?
faqs.answer: AcuityAds, a Canadian advertising technology company, acquired 140 Proof in August 2016 for up to $20 million.

faqs.question: What happened to the 140 Proof website?
faqs.answer: The 140proof.com domain now redirects toward pages associated with its acquirer's corporate site rather than an independent product.

faqs.question: What is illumin, and how does it relate to 140 Proof?
faqs.answer: Illumin is the current brand name of AcuityAds, the company that acquired 140 Proof in 2016; 140 Proof's targeting technology and team were folded into that company.

faqs.question: Who founded 140 Proof?
faqs.answer: 140 Proof was founded in 2009 by Jon Elvekrog and John Manoogian III.

faqs.question: Can I still sign up for 140 Proof today?
faqs.answer: No. There is no current signup flow or active product under the 140 Proof name; interested buyers would need to look at illumin instead.

enrichment.seo_title: 1984 FreeDNS Review: Free DNS Hosting From Iceland (2026)
enrichment.seo_meta_description: See what 1984's FreeDNS offers in 2026: unlimited free DNS zones, DNSSEC and dynamic DNS support, and how it fits into 1984's privacy-focused Icelandic hosting.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: 1984 is an Icelandic web hosting company founded in 2006 by a group of free software and civil liberties enthusiasts, headquartered in Reykjavík. The company has grown into one of Iceland's largest hosting providers, built around a stated mission of privacy, freedom of speech, and 100 percent renewable energy use, and it holds ISO 27001 information security certification. This listing covers 1984's FreeDNS product specifically, a free authoritative DNS hosting and management service available to anyone, whether or not they are a paying 1984 hosting customer. FreeDNS lets users register and manage as many DNS zones as they like with no artificial limits, and it supports a full range of DNS record types including A, AAAA, CAA, CNAME, MX, SRV, TXT, and SSHFP records. It also supports more advanced, security-oriented DNS features such as DNSSEC for cryptographically signed zones and TLSA records used for DANE-based certificate validation, features that are not always available in free DNS offerings from other providers. Additional capabilities include dynamic DNS support, which is useful for home servers or connections with changing IP addresses, and web request forwarding for redirecting traffic from a domain. 1984 states that FreeDNS has maintained 100 percent uptime for roughly two decades, and the company funds the free service through revenue from its paid hosting, VPS, and managed enterprise hosting offerings rather than charging directly for DNS. Because FreeDNS requires no purchase commitment, it functions as a genuinely free, standalone DNS tool that users can adopt regardless of where their website or email is hosted. It is best suited to individuals, small businesses, and self-hosting or home-lab enthusiasts who want reliable, full-featured DNS management without a subscription, particularly those who value working with a privacy-focused provider outside the major US cloud DNS providers. It is less suited to enterprises needing advanced traffic-steering features like GeoDNS, load-balancing based DNS responses, or a formal paid SLA, since those capabilities and support guarantees are more commonly found in commercial DNS platforms such as Cloudflare, AWS Route 53, or NS1.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is 1984 FreeDNS
paragraphs: 1984 is an Icelandic web hosting company founded in 2006, known for its focus on privacy, free speech, and renewable-energy-powered infrastructure. FreeDNS is the company's free authoritative DNS hosting product.
FreeDNS lets anyone register and manage DNS zones for their domains at no cost, without requiring them to purchase any other 1984 hosting service.
The service is aimed at individuals, small businesses, and technically minded users who want reliable, full-featured DNS management outside of the major commercial cloud DNS providers.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: FreeDNS supports an unlimited number of DNS zones per account and covers standard record types including A, AAAA, CAA, CNAME, MX, SRV, TXT, and SSHFP.
It also supports DNSSEC for signed, tamper-resistant DNS responses and TLSA records for DANE-based certificate validation, features not always found in free DNS tools.
Dynamic DNS support makes it useful for home servers with changing IP addresses, and web request forwarding allows simple domain redirects without additional infrastructure.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: FreeDNS is entirely free, with no zone limits and no requirement to purchase other 1984 hosting products.
1984 funds the free service through revenue from its paid web hosting, VPS, and managed enterprise hosting offerings.
There is no paid tier specifically for FreeDNS, and no published SLA beyond the company's stated long-term uptime track record.

features.title: Unlimited free DNS zones
features.description: Register and manage as many DNS zones as needed at no cost, with no artificial account limits.

features.title: Full standard record support
features.description: Supports A, AAAA, CAA, CNAME, MX, SRV, TXT, and SSHFP record types.

features.title: DNSSEC support
features.description: Cryptographically signed DNS zones help protect against DNS spoofing and cache poisoning.

features.title: TLSA and DANE support
features.description: Supports TLSA records for DANE-based TLS certificate validation.

features.title: Dynamic DNS
features.description: Useful for home servers or connections where the public IP address changes regularly.

features.title: Web request forwarding
features.description: Allows domain redirects to be configured directly through DNS management.

features.title: No purchase requirement
features.description: Available to anyone regardless of whether they use 1984's other hosting products.

features.title: Long uptime track record
features.description: 1984 reports roughly two decades of consistent uptime for the FreeDNS service.

pros: Completely free with no zone or domain limits
Backed by an established, privacy-focused Icelandic hosting company operating since 2006
Supports advanced records like DNSSEC and TLSA not always found in free DNS tools
Long-standing, reliable uptime track record
No requirement to be a paying 1984 hosting customer to use it
cons: No formal SLA or dedicated paid support tier specifically for FreeDNS
Interface is more utilitarian than modern, design-forward DNS dashboards
As a free service, support responsiveness may be slower than with paid DNS providers
Lacks advanced enterprise features like GeoDNS or load-balancing based DNS responses found in commercial DNS platforms

faqs.question: What is FreeDNS by 1984?
faqs.answer: FreeDNS is a free authoritative DNS hosting and management service offered by the Icelandic hosting company 1984, letting anyone manage DNS records for their domains at no cost.

faqs.question: Is FreeDNS really free?
faqs.answer: Yes, FreeDNS has no cost and no limit on the number of DNS zones a user can register and manage.

faqs.question: Do I need to be a 1984 hosting customer to use FreeDNS?
faqs.answer: No, FreeDNS is available to anyone and does not require purchasing any other 1984 hosting product.

faqs.question: Does FreeDNS support DNSSEC?
faqs.answer: Yes, FreeDNS supports DNSSEC for cryptographically signed DNS zones, along with TLSA records for DANE-based certificate validation.

faqs.question: Can I use FreeDNS for dynamic DNS?
faqs.answer: Yes, FreeDNS supports dynamic DNS, which is useful for home servers or connections with changing IP addresses.

faqs.question: How many domains can I add to FreeDNS?
faqs.answer: There is no stated limit; users can register and manage as many DNS zones as they like.

faqs.question: Who runs FreeDNS?
faqs.answer: FreeDNS is operated by 1984, an Icelandic web hosting company founded in 2006 and based in Reykjavík.

faqs.question: Is FreeDNS reliable?
faqs.answer: 1984 reports approximately two decades of consistent uptime for the FreeDNS service.

use_cases.title: Free DNS for personal and small business domains
use_cases.description: Individuals and small businesses use FreeDNS to manage DNS records for their domains without paying for a commercial DNS provider.

use_cases.description: Self-hosting enthusiasts use FreeDNS's dynamic DNS support to keep domains pointed at home servers with changing IP addresses.

use_cases.title: Privacy-focused DNS hosting
use_cases.description: Privacy-conscious users choose 1984's FreeDNS to keep their DNS management with a provider outside major US cloud platforms.

enrichment.short_description: AI-powered collaborative learning platform combining LMS and LXP capabilities for employee onboarding, compliance training, and skills development.
enrichment.starting_price: From $8 per user per month (Team plan, billed monthly, up to 100 users)
enrichment.headquarters: Paris, France, with additional offices in New York and London
enrichment.seo_meta_description: See what 360Learning offers in 2026: AI-powered collaborative learning, LMS and LXP features, HRIS integrations, and pricing plans starting at $8 per user.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: 360Learning is an AI-powered corporate learning platform founded in 2013 in Paris, France by Nick Hernandez and Guillaume Alary. The company has raised approximately $240 million in funding across multiple rounds, including a $200 million private equity round in 2021 led by Sumeru Equity Partners, SoftBank Vision Fund 2, and Silver Lake Waterman, with participation from existing investors Bpifrance Large Venture, XAnge, and Educapital. The company employs more than 300 people across offices in Paris, New York, and London and serves over 2,500 organizations, including Cisco, Duolingo, Michelin, LVMH, Safran, and Cognizant. 360Learning combines traditional learning management system (LMS) functionality with learning experience platform (LXP) capabilities, and it is built around what the company calls collaborative learning: enabling subject matter experts within an organization to create and refine training content directly, rather than relying solely on top-down instructional design. Key product capabilities include an AI Content Builder that helps non-instructional-designer employees turn expertise into interactive courses and scenarios while keeping L&D teams in an oversight role, a Data and Insights module that analyzes performance, detects skill gaps, recommends courses, and automates reporting, and an AI Companion that delivers personalized content recommendations and real-time coaching across mobile and desktop. The platform integrates with more than 80 HRIS and HCM systems, including Workday, SAP, BambooHR, and Slack, to sync employee and organizational data. Use cases span compliance training, employee onboarding, sales enablement, frontline staff training, and external customer or partner training, all served from a single platform that can support multiple audiences at once. 360Learning has been named a top LMS and AI LMS in industry rankings for 2026 and holds GDPR, ISO 27001, and SOC 2 certifications. The company also runs a community called The L&D Collective, with more than 4,000 members, and an L&D Performance Academy that has certified more than 6,000 learners. Pricing starts with a Team plan at $8 per user per month, billed monthly, covering up to 100 users, while Business and Enterprise tiers are sold through custom sales quotes based on scope and requirements; a 30-day free trial is available with no setup fees.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is 360Learning
paragraphs: 360Learning is an AI-powered learning platform founded in 2013 in Paris, France, combining LMS and learning experience platform (LXP) capabilities in one product.

The company pioneered collaborative learning, an approach where subject matter experts inside an organization help create and refine training content, rather than relying only on a centralized L&D team.
It serves more than 2,500 organizations globally, including large enterprises like Cisco, Duolingo, and Michelin, and has raised roughly $240 million in funding to support its growth.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: 360Learning's AI Content Builder helps internal experts turn their knowledge into interactive courses and scenarios while L&D teams retain oversight and quality control.
Its Data and Insights tools analyze learner performance, detect skill gaps, recommend relevant courses, and automate reporting for L&D and HR leaders.

The platform integrates with more than 80 HRIS and HCM systems, including Workday, SAP, and BambooHR, and supports multiple training audiences, from employees to customers and partners, on a single platform.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: 360Learning's Team plan starts at $8 per user per month, billed monthly, and covers up to 100 active users with no hidden setup fees or minimum volume commitments.
Business and Enterprise tiers are priced through a custom sales quote based on company size, integration needs, and scope of use.
A 30-day free trial is available for organizations wanting to evaluate the platform before committing to a paid plan.

enrichment.short_description: Free cloud-based code snippet manager that lets developers store, organize, and share code snippets in real-time collaborative three-column boards.
enrichment.seo_title: 3Cols Review: Free Cloud Code Snippet Manager (2026)
enrichment.seo_meta_description: See what 3Cols offers in 2026: a free cloud code snippet manager with three-column boards, real-time collaboration, and VSCode and browser extensions.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: 3Cols is a free, cloud-based code snippet manager built by developer Oli Blade. Despite a name that might suggest a project management or kanban tool, 3Cols is specifically a tool for storing, organizing, and sharing reusable code snippets, and its name refers to the three-column board layout used to structure those snippets visually rather than to a kanban-style task workflow. The site's own metadata describes it as a way to get 'your code snippets instantly, anywhere, with anyone,' and third-party listings on platforms like Product Hunt, AlternativeTo, and SourceForge describe it as a cloud snippet manager for personal and collaborative code. Snippets created in 3Cols are stored in the cloud and accessible from any device with an internet connection. The three-column board interface lets users structure and organize snippets, and boards can be shared privately with individuals or teams for real-time collaborative editing, so a group can jointly build and maintain a shared library of reusable code. The platform supports syntax highlighting and organization for roughly 140 programming languages. Beyond the core web app, 3Cols offers a browser extension that lets users capture code blocks directly from web pages they are browsing, and an official VSCode extension, published on GitHub as 3ColsVSCode, that lets developers insert, update, and create 3Cols snippets, including snippets from boards shared with them, directly inside their code editor. The web app is also built as a Progressive Web App, meaning it can be installed as a standalone application on Windows and Android devices in addition to being used in a browser. 3Cols does not charge for storing, accessing, or sharing snippets, and no paid tier is publicly advertised, making it a genuinely free tool rather than a freemium product with hidden upsells. Public information about the company behind 3Cols is limited: there is no widely published founding year, headquarters, or team size, suggesting it is a smaller, independently run or solo-developer project rather than a venture-backed company. This makes it best suited to individual developers and small teams looking for a lightweight, free way to organize and share code snippets, rather than to large enterprises seeking a fully supported, SLA-backed developer tool.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is 3Cols
paragraphs: 3Cols is a free, cloud-based code snippet manager that helps developers store, organize, and share reusable code snippets. Its name comes from the three-column board layout used to organize snippets, not from any kanban or project management functionality.
Snippets are stored in the cloud and accessible from any device, and boards can be shared privately with individuals or teams for real-time collaborative editing.

The tool is built by independent developer Oli Blade, and public information about the company behind it, such as a founding year or headquarters, is limited, suggesting it is a small or independently run project.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: 3Cols organizes code into three-column boards with support for roughly 140 programming languages and syntax highlighting.
A browser extension lets users capture code snippets directly from web pages, while an official VSCode extension allows inserting, updating, and creating 3Cols snippets from inside the code editor.
The web app is built as a Progressive Web App, so it can be installed as a standalone application on Windows and Android in addition to running in a browser.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: 3Cols is completely free, with no cost to store, access, or share snippets.
No paid tier or premium plan is publicly advertised on the platform.
This makes it accessible to individual developers or small teams without any billing commitment.

enrichment.short_description: API management platform for securing, publishing, and monetizing APIs across hybrid cloud, now developed and sold by Red Hat as Red Hat 3scale API Management.
enrichment.headquarters: Originally Barcelona, Spain; now part of Red Hat, headquartered in Raleigh, North Carolina, United States
enrichment.seo_title: 3scale Review: Red Hat API Management Platform (2026)
enrichment.seo_meta_description: See what 3scale (now Red Hat 3scale API Management) offers in 2026: API gateway, developer portal, monetization, and hybrid cloud deployment features.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: 3scale is an API management platform originally co-founded in 2007 by Steven Willmott and Martin Tantow in Barcelona, Spain. The company launched a public beta in November 2008 and released its commercial product, 3scale Connect, in March 2009, offering both a self-serve version and a premium version with support and a service-level agreement. It grew through the early 2010s, raising $4.2 million in funding in March 2013 to expand functionality and international reach. 3scale and Red Hat began partnering in early 2015, building a joint solution that combined 3scale's API management with Red Hat's existing middleware portfolio, and in 2016 Red Hat fully acquired 3scale to strengthen its ability to manage the growing number of public and internal APIs being built on containers and microservices. Since the acquisition, 3scale has operated as Red Hat 3scale API Management, part of the broader Red Hat Integration product suite, and Red Hat itself is now a subsidiary of IBM. The original 3scale.net domain now redirects to Red Hat's product page, reflecting that 3scale no longer exists as an independently branded, separately sold company. Functionally, Red Hat 3scale API Management provides an API gateway for traffic control, rate limiting, and access policy enforcement; centralized API program management covering analytics, access control, and monetization; and a developer portal for publishing APIs and onboarding external or internal developers. It is built for hybrid cloud deployment, supporting cloud, on-premise, or combined environments, and integrates closely with Red Hat OpenShift and Kubernetes for organizations building containerized, microservices-based applications. Security capabilities include authentication, authorization, and encryption for API traffic. As part of Red Hat's enterprise portfolio, 3scale does not publish self-serve pricing; prospective customers must contact Red Hat sales or use a product trial to get cost information, and pricing is generally bundled into broader Red Hat subscription and support agreements. The platform is best suited to enterprises, particularly those already invested in Red Hat and OpenShift, that need a mature, well-supported API management layer spanning gateway, analytics, developer experience, and monetization, and is less suited to small teams or startups looking for a lightweight, self-serve, cloud-native API gateway.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is 3scale
paragraphs: 3scale was an independent API management company founded in 2007 in Barcelona, Spain, that built tools for securing, publishing, analyzing, and monetizing APIs.
After a partnership beginning in 2015, Red Hat acquired 3scale in 2016, and the product now operates as Red Hat 3scale API Management, part of the Red Hat Integration suite.
The original 3scale.net website now redirects to Red Hat's product pages, and Red Hat, which is itself owned by IBM, is now the sole vendor and support provider for the platform.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The platform includes an API gateway for traffic control, rate limiting, and access policy enforcement, along with centralized program management covering analytics, access control, and monetization.
A developer portal helps organizations publish APIs and onboard internal or external developer consumers.
It supports hybrid cloud deployment models and integrates closely with Red Hat OpenShift and Kubernetes for containerized, microservices-based environments.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Red Hat 3scale API Management does not publish self-serve pricing on its product pages.
Prospective customers must contact Red Hat sales directly or use a product trial to obtain cost information.
Pricing is typically bundled into broader Red Hat enterprise subscription and support agreements rather than sold as a fully standalone product.

Backed by a large, established vendor for long-term product stability
cons: No self-serve public pricing; requires contacting Red Hat sales for a quote

More complex to deploy and manage than lightweight, cloud-native API gateways

faqs.question: What is 3scale?
faqs.answer: 3scale is an API management platform originally founded in 2007, now sold and developed by Red Hat as Red Hat 3scale API Management.

faqs.question: Is 3scale still an independent company?
faqs.answer: No. 3scale was acquired by Red Hat in 2016 and now operates fully as part of Red Hat's product portfolio.

faqs.question: How much does 3scale cost?
faqs.answer: Red Hat does not publish self-serve pricing for 3scale; customers must contact Red Hat sales for a custom quote.

faqs.question: Does 3scale work with Kubernetes or OpenShift?
faqs.answer: Yes, 3scale integrates closely with Red Hat OpenShift and Kubernetes for containerized, microservices-based deployments.

faqs.question: What happened to 3scale.net?
faqs.answer: The original 3scale.net domain now redirects to Red Hat's Red Hat 3scale API Management product page.

faqs.question: Who founded 3scale?
faqs.answer: 3scale was co-founded by Steven Willmott and Martin Tantow in 2007 in Barcelona, Spain.

faqs.question: What does 3scale do for API monetization?
faqs.answer: 3scale provides tools for tracking API usage and enabling usage-based billing and monetization of API products.

faqs.question: Is 3scale open source?
faqs.answer: 3scale offers an open-source API gateway component, while the full management platform is sold as a commercial Red Hat product with paid support.
